annotations.label: Circular motion

anchor.snippet: circular motion is movement of an object along a circle or two-dimensional rotation along a circular arc

annotations.note: `circleMap c R θ = c + R·exp(θ·i)` parametrizes a circular path, but Mathlib has no notion of time-evolution motion of an object.

annotations.label: Examples of circular motion

anchor.snippet: Examples of circular motion include: special satellite orbits around the Earth

annotations.note: Physical examples like satellite orbits are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Centripetal acceleration from changing velocity direction

anchor.snippet: the moving object is undergoing acceleration by a centripetal force in the direction of the center of rotation

annotations.note: Centripetal force/acceleration is a physics concept absent from Mathlib (loogle finds no `centripetal` decls).

annotations.label: Uniform circular motion

anchor.snippet: uniform circular motion describes the motion of a body traversing a circular path at a constant speed

annotations.note: `circleMap c R θ` with linear `θ` is mathematically equivalent to uniform circular motion, but Mathlib does not name it as such.

annotations.label: Rigid body rotation about a fixed axis

anchor.snippet: each particle of the body describes a uniform circular motion with the same angular velocity

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of rigid bodies or axis-of-rotation kinematics.

annotations.label: Angular velocity from period

anchor.snippet: If the period for one rotation is T , the angular rate of rotation, also known as angular velocity

annotations.note: The relation ω = 2π/T is a physics definition not present in Mathlib (no `angularVelocity` decls).

annotations.label: Speed on the circle

anchor.snippet: The speed of the object traveling the circle is

annotations.note: Speed v = 2πr/T as a physics quantity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Angle swept in time t

anchor.snippet: The angle θ swept out in a time t is

annotations.note: The kinematic identity θ = ωt is not a Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Angular acceleration

anchor.snippet: The angular acceleration , α , of the particle is

annotations.note: Angular acceleration α = dω/dt is a physics quantity absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zero angular acceleration in uniform circular motion

anchor.snippet: In the case of uniform circular motion, α will be zero

annotations.note: Depends on the absent notion of angular acceleration.

annotations.label: Centripetal acceleration formula

anchor.snippet: The acceleration due to change in the direction is

annotations.note: The formula a = v²/r = ω²r as a physics statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Centripetal force from Newton's second law

anchor.snippet: for a body of mass m , the centripetal force is, by Newton's second law

annotations.note: Newton's second law and force are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unit body in unit circle at unit angular velocity

anchor.snippet: Consider a body of one kilogram, moving in a circle of radius one metre, with an angular velocity of one radian per second

annotations.note: A physical worked example with units; not formalized.

annotations.label: Velocity as cross product of angular velocity and position

anchor.snippet: the velocity is given by a vector cross product

annotations.note: Mathlib has `crossProduct` but no formulation of v = ω × r for circular motion.

annotations.label: Acceleration as cross product of angular velocity and velocity

anchor.snippet: Likewise, the acceleration is given by

annotations.note: The identity a = ω × v for circular motion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Displacement vector in polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: the radial vector from the origin to the particle location

annotations.note: `polarCoord.symm (r,θ) = (r cos θ, r sin θ)` gives the radial position vector, though not framed as a moving particle.

annotations.label: Velocity in polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: The velocity is the time derivative of the displacement

annotations.note: The Fréchet derivative of `polarCoord.symm` provides the underlying calculus but not the kinematic decomposition into radial/tangential velocity components.

annotations.label: Derivative of the radial unit vector

anchor.snippet: If the particle displacement rotates through an angle dθ in time dt

annotations.note: `deriv_circleMap` gives d/dθ (c + R e^{iθ}) = R e^{iθ}·i, which when R=1 is exactly the rotated radial unit vector.

annotations.label: Acceleration in polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: The acceleration is the time derivative of the velocity

annotations.note: The polar-coordinate acceleration decomposition with radial and tangential components is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Centripetal and tangential components of acceleration

anchor.snippet: The centripetal acceleration is the radial component, which is directed radially inward

annotations.note: Kinematic decomposition of acceleration is not formalized.

annotations.label: Position as a complex number

anchor.snippet: Circular motion can be described using complex numbers and Euler's formula

annotations.note: `circleMap c R θ = c + R·exp(θ·i)` is exactly the complex-number parametrization given in the article.

annotations.label: Velocity and acceleration in complex form

anchor.snippet: With this notation, the velocity becomes

annotations.note: `deriv_circleMap` yields velocity i·R·e^{iθ}; the second derivative (acceleration −R·e^{iθ}) is not separately named.

annotations.label: Speed equals angular rate times radius

anchor.snippet: the distance traveled on the periphery of the orbit is s = rθ

annotations.note: Arc-length relation s = rθ and v = rω for a moving particle is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Magnitude of centripetal acceleration from geometric construction

anchor.snippet: the change in v is a vector at right angles to v and of magnitude v dθ

annotations.note: Geometric derivation of |a| = v²/r is a physics argument not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proper acceleration for relativistic circular motion

anchor.snippet: the three-acceleration vector is perpendicular to the three-velocity vector

annotations.note: Special relativity kinematics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-uniform circular motion

anchor.snippet: In non-uniform circular motion , an object moves in a circular path with varying speed

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize variable-speed circular trajectories as a named concept.

annotations.label: Net acceleration directed into circle interior

anchor.snippet: The net acceleration is directed towards the interior of the circle

annotations.note: Geometric/physical claim about acceleration direction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Decomposition into tangential and centripetal acceleration

anchor.snippet: The net acceleration may be resolved into two components: tangential acceleration and centripetal acceleration

annotations.note: Acceleration-decomposition kinematics not present.

annotations.label: Normal force as sum of radial and tangential forces

anchor.snippet: The normal force is actually the sum of the radial and tangential forces

annotations.note: Force decomposition is a physics notion absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formulas for speed, acceleration and jerk in non-uniform circular motion

anchor.snippet: One can deduce the formulae of speed, acceleration and jerk

annotations.note: Higher kinematic quantities (jerk) and these formulas are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum of forces equals centripetal force

anchor.snippet: the sum of all the forces acting on the object will have to be equal to the centripetal force

annotations.note: Newtonian force-balance statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Total acceleration as vector sum in non-uniform circular motion

anchor.snippet: find the vector sum of the tangential acceleration and the radial acceleration

annotations.note: Vector sum of kinematic components not formalized.

annotations.label: Tangential acceleration as derivative of speed

anchor.snippet: Tangential acceleration is simply the derivative of the speed at any given point

annotations.note: Defines a physics quantity (tangential acceleration) not present in Mathlib.
